title: The Primes Contain Arbitrarily Long Arithmetic Progressions
citation: Ben Green and Terence Tao, Annals of Mathematics, 167(2):481–547, 2008
license: not assessed; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Landmark Annals paper establishing the Green–Tao theorem on arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions in the primes.
notes: Selected for theorem statements, notation, and proof-transition prose.

title: User's Guide to Viscosity Solutions of Second Order Partial Differential Equations
citation: Michael G. Crandall, Hitoshi Ishii, and Pierre-Louis Lions, Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society, 27(1):1–67, 1992
access: author manuscript on arXiv; publisher metadata available
venue: Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society
license: not assessed; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Canonical field guide that systematizes the modern viscosity-solution framework for second-order partial differential equations.
notes: Selected for formal definitions, assumptions, equivalence, and comparison-proof prose.

title: The No-U-Turn Sampler: Adaptively Setting Path Lengths in Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
citation: Matthew D. Hoffman and Andrew Gelman, Journal of Machine Learning Research, 15(47):1593–1623, 2014
license: not assessed; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Method paper for NUTS, which authoritative Stan documentation identifies as its default sampling algorithm.
notes: Selected for stochastic notation, algorithm initialization, recurrence, and convergence language.

title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
citation: Diederik P. Kingma and Max Welling, International Conference on Learning Representations, 2014
license: not assessed; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Winner of the inaugural ICLR Test of Time Award for long-lasting impact on variational autoencoders and scalable probabilistic inference.
notes: Selected for equivalent variational forms, reparameterization, optimization, and algorithm prose.

title: Distributed Optimization and Statistical Learning via the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers
citation: Stephen Boyd, Neal Parikh, Eric Chu, Borja Peleato, and Jonathan Eckstein, Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning, 3(1):1–122, 2011
venue: Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning
version: author manuscript corresponding to journal article
license: not assessed; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Authoritative modern reference that consolidated ADMM and its applications across distributed optimization and statistical learning.
notes: Selected for role-complete optimization statements, equivalence boundaries, and update descriptions.

title: Geometric Tracking Control of a Quadrotor UAV on SE(3)
citation: Taeyoung Lee, Melvin Leok, and N. Harris McClamroch, Proceedings of the 49th IEEE Conference on Decision and Control, pp. 5420–5425, 2010
access: archival conference paper and author publication record
venue: 49th IEEE Conference on Decision and Control
license: not assessed; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Field-recognized early geometric-control formulation for quadrotor trajectory tracking directly on SE(3), with a globally defined model and almost-global closed-loop analysis.
notes: Selected for frame-aware notation, governing dynamics, error definitions, and stability claims.

title: Robust Uncertainty Principles: Exact Signal Reconstruction from Highly Incomplete Frequency Information
citation: Emmanuel J. Candès, Justin Romberg, and Terence Tao, IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 52(2):489–509, 2006
access: author manuscript on arXiv; publisher metadata available
license: not assessed; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Foundational compressed-sensing result connecting exact sparse recovery from incomplete Fourier data with convex optimization.
notes: Selected for transforms, sparse representations, convex programs, probability-qualified recovery, and theorem prose.

title: A Mathematical Theory of Communication
citation: Claude E. Shannon, Bell System Technical Journal, 27:379–423 and 623–656, 1948
access: publisher metadata and institutional reading copy
version: version of record in two parts
license: not assessed; no source text redistributed
influence_basis: Seminal paper that established the mathematical foundations of information theory and modern communication systems.
notes: Selected for explicit definitions, theorem-to-definition transitions, interpretation, and substitution prose.

summary: Names the prime-counting function immediately after the displayed density condition.
Attributes a stronger counting statement to a prior proposition plus an additional combinatorial argument.
Introduces viscosity subsolutions only after motivating the test-function and superjet conditions.
Names all premises that jointly license the comparison inequality before displaying it.
Defines symbols and their physical roles immediately after the joint-density display.
Introduces a three-stage numerical recurrence as the concrete propagation rule.
Sets the sampler endpoints, momenta, depth, candidate set, and stop flag from the current Markov state.
Moves from the marginal likelihood identity to two equivalent expressions for the variational lower bound.
Parameterizes a conditional random variable by a deterministic map of auxiliary noise and observed data.
Identifies the lower bound as the objective and distinguishes variational from generative decision parameters.
States objective, equality constraint, decision variables, dimensions, and convexity assumptions around one display.
Interprets the displayed recurrence as two ordered minimizations followed by a dual update.
Justifies equivalence by noting that the added penalty vanishes at every feasible point.

locator: Sec. II, model-variable definition block before Eq. (1)
cue: define ... the rotation matrix from ... to ...
summary: Introduces each model variable together with its mathematical type, physical role, and coordinate frame before stating the dynamics.

cue: equations of motion ... can be written as
summary: Introduces translational and rotational governing equations after fixing frames, types, and actuator conventions.

cue: define the tracking errors ... as follows
summary: Defines position, velocity, attitude, and angular-velocity tracking errors while explaining why tangent-space quantities require frame-aware comparison.
Supplies a sparse spike representation only after stating the additional support assumption.
Introduces the recovery program through its decision variable, objective norm, and Fourier-data constraint.
Supplies an explicit limiting expression after the capacity concept and operational interpretation are established.

locator: Part I, Sec. 6, conditional entropy derivation
summary: Substitutes the conditional-probability expression into the entropy definition to obtain an equivalent expanded identity.

intent: Assign notation to an already identified mathematical object.
boundaries: Keep the symbol-to-object direction explicit.
Do not imply that naming supplies an internal formula.
Place local denotations near first semantic use.
synthetic_example: Let n(A) denote the number of admissible elements in A.

intent: Introduce a manuscript-level definition after its domain and motivating conditions are clear.
constructions: we are now ready to define ...
boundaries: State the domain and required conditions before or with the definition.
Do not use define for a relation merely inherited from cited theory.
Separate motivation from the formal defining condition.
synthetic_example: For x in X, define the residual map by r(x):=Ax-b.

intent: Supply an explicit expression for an object already introduced by role.
boundaries: Ensure the object is already identifiable in the discourse.
Preserve assumptions that license the representation.
Mark approximation explicitly instead of using an equality frame.
synthetic_example: Under the sparsity assumption, the signal can be written as a finite sum of basis elements.

intent: Introduce the exact result of substituting one displayed relation into another.
boundaries: Name or cite the relation being substituted.
Preserve domain and nonzero conditions.
Do not use deduction language for a merely mechanical algebraic step.
synthetic_example: Substituting the feedback law into the state equation gives the closed-loop dynamics.

intent: Present a mathematically equivalent form while explaining what licenses equivalence.
boundaries: Require both directions on the stated domain.
Explain why added or removed terms do not change feasible values when that is the basis of equivalence.
Use implies rather than equivalently for a one-way result.
synthetic_example: Because the penalty vanishes on the feasible set, the constrained problem is equivalent to its augmented form.

intent: Mark a logical consequence while keeping its premises visible.
boundaries: List the premises or cite the result that entails the conclusion.
Do not use deduction markers as decorative transitions.
Preserve the direction and strength of the implication.
synthetic_example: Combining the feasibility condition with the bound, we deduce that the residual converges to zero.

intent: Describe a discrete update while preserving evaluation order and state semantics.
boundaries: State the iteration index and step size when relevant.
Distinguish sequential from simultaneous updates.
Identify which variables form the persistent state and which are intermediate.
synthetic_example: The iteration consists of a primal minimization followed by a multiplier update.

intent: Introduce an optimization problem with its mathematical roles explicit.
boundaries: Identify decision variables, objective, constraints, and given data.
State convexity or existence assumptions only when established.
Do not call a displayed candidate optimal without a solution argument.
synthetic_example: Given y, estimate x by minimizing J(x;y) over x in X subject to g(x)<=0.

intent: Introduce a governing law after the state, frame, and inputs are fixed.
constructions: the equations of motion can be written as
boundaries: State continuous or discrete time explicitly.
Preserve coordinate-frame conventions and initial conditions.
Do not confuse a numerical update rule with the physical governing law.
synthetic_example: In the inertial frame, the state evolves according to x_dot=f(x,u).
